literal: that which holds, supports, sustains; from √dhṛ 'to hold, to bear'
invariant_definition: *Dharma* is a load-bearing term across ritual theory, jurisprudence, ontology, yoga, and Buddhist phenomenology. The root sense is 'that which upholds,' but philosophical use immediately splits into several linked registers. In Mīmāṃsā, *dharma* is what is known through Vedic injunction and enacted through prescribed performance; the debate then turns on whether an act is *kratvartha* or *puruṣārtha*, and on how unseen efficacy connects action and result. In epic and social discourse, *dharma* becomes the right ordering of role, relation, and circumstance. In Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika, a *dharma* of a *dharmin* is a property borne by a substance. In Buddhism, *dharma* or *dhamma* can name both the teaching and the elementary factors analyzed as the constituents of experience. Vedānta inherits all these uses but reorders them around liberation: which *dharma* binds, which purifies, which continues as devotion, and whether the highest state lies beyond enjoinment or fulfills it.

per_school.definition: Within Advaita, *svadharma* belongs to the preparatory economy of *adhikāra* and purification rather than to the immediate production of *mokṣa* [[BhR 1.1 ṭīkā 12](<cite://madhusudana/bhakti-rasayana/1.1 ṭīkā.12>)]. Madhusūdana accordingly cites the Bhāgavata to show that exclusive Hari-bhajana outranks mere perseverance in one’s own duty, since dharma pursued as karma does not by itself culminate in the highest end [[BhR 1.1 ṭīkā 12](<cite://madhusudana/bhakti-rasayana/1.1 ṭīkā.12>)].

*Dharma* retains binding reality because śāstra assigns distinct yet non-conflicting domains to Vedic and Pāñcarātra injunction, and Vedānta Deśika speaks of a normatively settled *dharma* governing their proper employment [[PR 1, Avirodhopayogābhyām](cite://vedanta-desika/pancaratra-raksha/1.vaikhānasa-pāñcarātra-avirodha)]. At the existential level, Yāmuna treats mere *dharma-niṣṭhā* as insufficient unless it matures into surrender at the Lord’s feet, so dharma is preserved but gathered into *prapatti* and *bhagavad-śeṣatva* [[YStr 22-24](cite://yamuna/stotra-ratna/22-24)].

Madhva uses *dharma* with full realist force: the *pakṣa*, *sapakṣa*, and *vipakṣa* are each specified through a borne property, so inferential practice tracks real *dharma*-relations rather than merely conceptual imputations [[MUK 239.15](<cite://madhva/mithyatvanumana-khandana/239, clause 15>)] [[MUK 239.16](<cite://madhva/mithyatvanumana-khandana/239, clause 16>)] [[MUK 239.17](<cite://madhva/mithyatvanumana-khandana/239, clause 17>)]. The same realism governs his anti-Buddhist polemic: the fault lies in treating *dharmas* as self-standing items, whereas their intelligibility depends upon the higher order of Viṣṇu’s governance and not upon isolated factor-ontology [[VTV 3.21](cite://madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.21)].

In Vallabha’s ontology, *dharma* is retained in the strong sense of a real property: *nāma-rūpa* are *kārya-dharmas*, predicates of the manifested effect and not illusory overlays on Brahman [[BSB 1.1.4](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.4)] [[AB 1.1.4d](cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.1.4d)]. Because the effect is Brahman’s own manifestation under *avikṛta-pariṇāma*, dharma-language marks real determinations within manifestation without compromising pure non-duality [[BSB 1.1.4](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.4)] [[AB 1.1.4d](cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.1.4d)].

In Sāṃkhya, *dharma* is one of the *bhāvas* that inhabit the *liṅga* and condition transmigration, together with jñāna, vairāgya, and aiśvarya [[SK 40](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/40)] [[SK 43](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/43)]. Its causal valence is explicit: dharma carries the stream upward, adharma downward, and only jñāna terminates the process in *apavarga* [[SK 44](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/44)].

In Yoga, *dharma* names the determinable trait-structure through which a *dharmī* is followed across change; the transformations of property, temporal mark, and state are generalized from citta to the elements and senses [[YS 3.13](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/3.13)] [[YS 3.14](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/3.14)]. At the soteriological limit, however, the culminating concentration is *dharma-megha-samādhi*, where even attachment to discriminative knowledge is relinquished and the path stands on the threshold of final release [[YS 4.29](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/4.29)].

Kaṇāda opens Vaiśeṣika by defining *dharma* as that from which *abhyudaya* and *niḥśreyasa* are accomplished, so the term first names a real causal-normative principle and not merely social duty [[VS 1.1.1](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.1.1)] [[VS 1.1.2](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.1.2)]. Within the padārtha-analysis that follows, explanatory order is carried by *dravya*, *guṇa*, and *karma*; a guṇa is an *aguṇavat* attribute resting in a substance, which is why Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika can speak naturally of a *dharma* of a *dharmin* without leaving its ontological register [[VS 1.1.15](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.1.15)] [[VS 1.1.16](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.1.16)].

Jaimini fixes *dharma* as the very object of inquiry: one turns to Mīmāṃsā not toward a highest substance but toward the ascertainment of what is to be done [[MS 1.1.1](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.1.1)]. Its lakṣaṇa is *codanā-lakṣaṇo 'rtho dharmaḥ*, and because dharma is rooted in *śabda* the non-verbal is not to be expected as an independent source for it [[MS 1.1.2](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.1.2)] [[MS 1.3.1](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.3.1)].

Nāgārjuna presses the term *dharma* to the point of de-reification: no dharma can be shown to arise as existent, non-existent, or both, and therefore no substantialist model of causal support survives analysis [[MMK 1.7](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.7)] [[MMK 1.8](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.8)] [[MMK 1.9](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.9)]. Buddha-dharma itself is then articulated through the two truths: conventionally there is *dharmadeśanā*, but ultimately no dharma arises independently and therefore none is non-empty [[MMK 24.8](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.8)] [[MMK 24.12](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.12)] [[MMK 24.19](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.19)]. When all dharmas are empty, nirvāṇa too is the pacification of all proliferations rather than the disclosure of a final dharma-substrate [[MMK 25.22](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.22)] [[MMK 25.24](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/25.24)] [[MMK 18.7](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.7)].

Vasubandhu treats ordinary imputations of self and dharma as moments internal to *vijñāna-pariṇāma*; what appears as a field of discrete dharmas is already representation-only [[Triṃś 1](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/1)]. The school’s decisive move is *dharma-nairātmya*: the grāhya-grāhaka structure imagined in dharmas is denied as *kalpitātman*, not by abolishing all appearance but by redescribing it through the three natures and the threefold *niḥsvabhāvatā* [[Viṃś 10](<cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/10 and Entry into *Dharma-Nairātmya*>)] [[Triṃś 23](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/23)]. On that basis the ultimate among dharmas is *tathatā*, while the consummation of the path is spoken of as *dharmakāya* [[Triṃś 25](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/25)] [[Triṃś 30](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/30)].

Jainism gives *dharma* a sharply technical sense: alongside *adharma*, *ākāśa*, and *pudgala*, it is one of the *ajīva-kāyas* and therefore one of the real dravyas of the cosmos [[TS 5.1](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/5.1)] [[TS 5.2](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/5.2)]. Its distinctive function is not merit but motion-medium: *dharma* aids movement, *adharma* aids rest, and the liberated soul’s upward movement is possible because the *dharmāstikāya* exists [[TS 5.17](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/5.17)] [[TS 10.8](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/10.8)].

translator_note: Avoid the lazy glosses 'religion,' 'duty,' and 'law' when used without qualification. 'Religion' imports a modern confessional category; 'duty' narrows the term to ethics; 'law' can make it sound merely juridical. *Dharma* names whatever upholds a normative, ontological, or soteriological order, and context decides which register is active.
